group.text: For the first few days, space feels quieter than any of us expected. The ship moves forward without asking much from us, cutting through the dark with three colored trails behind it. Somewhere ahead is Earth, still too far to see, but close enough to make everything feel real. We are JIDLI: Jiroh, Depimi, and Lebang. Three idols from another world, sent to make first contact through music, stories, and whatever parts of ourselves survive the journey. Back home, they called it a mission. Out here, it feels more like a question waiting to open.

We have only known each other for a few days, which is strange when you are sharing a room in the middle of space. None of this feels normal yet. The ship hums at night, the window never turns fully dark, and everyone is pretending they know how to sleep while flying toward another planet. We still have to learn each other properly. What makes someone laugh, what makes them quiet, what they do when fear starts showing. For now, we notice the small things. The tired eyes, the forced smiles, the way nobody admits they are nervous first.

There is not much to do on a spaceship, so we spend a lot of time together. Not because we planned some deep bonding moment, but because space gives you very few places to dramatically disappear. Most nights, we end up under the ceiling window, staring at stars that do not look any closer no matter how long we watch them. This is where we start becoming real to each other. Not idols, not chosen representatives, just three people trying to understand what comes next. Somewhere between the silence and the stars, we make a quiet promise: stay ourselves.

The briefing arrives the next day. No dramatic entrance, no warning, just a file opening on the ship’s system like it has been waiting there the whole time. At first, we think it is the plan for first contact. Then we actually read it. Styling references, approved poses, suggested slogans, emotional tone: friendly, simple, marketable. Every part of us has been translated into something easy to sell before we have even reached Earth. Jiroh goes quiet first. Then Depimi. Then Lebang. The ship keeps moving, but for the first time, the mission starts to feel wrong.

For a while, nobody says anything. The file stays open, glowing politely, as if it has not just insulted every reason we were chosen. There are no stories in it. No risk. No strange little parts of us that made the journey worth taking. Just a polished version of JIDLI that could belong to anyone. Depimi looks hurt first, which somehow makes the rest of us angrier. Then Jiroh says, very calmly, “No.” Lebang smiles like a storm is arriving. We do not need a vote after that. The official plan ends right there.

Once we decide to ignore the official plan, the room stops feeling peaceful. We have ideas, too many of them, and none of them agree at first. Colors clash. Shapes fight. Everyone wants the concept to feel honest, but honesty is not automatically pretty. Jiroh takes the outfits seriously, maybe too seriously, because fabric is his language. Still, designing for three bodies, three moods, three histories, is harder than he expects. Depimi starts sketching beside him, translating the mess into something clearer. It is not perfect yet. That is the point. At least now, it is ours.

Lebang and Depimi take over the music next. He understands rhythm like it lives in his bones. She finds words for feelings before anyone else can name them. At first, they work quietly, sitting across from each other under the observation window, passing ideas back and forth like they are afraid to break them. Then the song starts opening. A beat becomes a melody, a melody becomes a sentence, and suddenly they are both smiling without noticing. It is small, almost hidden, but something changes there. The song feels warmer because they are making it together.

After hours of working, we sit down for dinner together, close enough for knees to bump and chopsticks to get stolen by accident. At first, everyone is tired in that serious way people get when they care too much. Then one joke slips out, then another, and suddenly the whole room feels lighter. Something changes when you create with people. You stop performing politeness and start showing the weird parts by accident. Jiroh gets sharper when he feels safe. Depimi laughs louder than expected. Lebang is impossible to insult. Slowly, the ship starts feeling less like transport and more like home.

The outside of the album takes shape slowly. We want it to hold more than music, more than photos, more than proof that we made it here. It has to hold the truth of us. Not the polished version from the briefing, but the real one we are still figuring out how to tell. Jiroh works through the structure while Lebang keeps turning the idea back to the story itself. Then it clicks. It should look like a book. Of course it should. If someone is going to open our world, they should feel like they are opening our story.

The more real the project becomes, the easier it is to doubt it. Depimi is the first one who goes quiet in a way that feels different from her usual quiet. She stands by the window, staring into space like the answer might be written somewhere between the stars. What if we are wrong? What if Earth does not understand us? What if the official plan was safer for a reason? She does not say all of it out loud, but we can feel it. She keeps thinking about home, wondering what her sisters would tell her to do.

Jiroh stays up later than the rest of us, finishing details most people would never notice. A stitch here, a knot there, one last adjustment until the outfits finally look alive under his hands. He knows they are good. That almost makes it worse. Good means people might look closer. Good means this could actually matter. For a second, the room feels too quiet without his sister in it, without her voice telling him when to stop overthinking and sleep. He keeps working anyway, because missing someone does not pause the mission. It just makes his hands move slower.

Lebang’s doubt comes out differently. He does not freeze, and he does not say much. He just takes the ship plans and studies them like the answer might appear if he stares hard enough. To pull this off, we need to record everything before the system locks us out and the official files take over again. Suddenly, it is not just about dancing well or singing loudly. It is timing, routes, power, access. He keeps tracing the same corridor with his finger. His brother would know what to do. Lebang breathes once, then decides he will too.

The countdown appears across the main screen exactly twelve hours before the official recording begins. The ship announces it politely, like this is just another scheduled task. Twelve hours until the approved song files open. Twelve hours until the cameras activate. Twelve hours until JIDLI is supposed to become the version the briefing invented. The system does not know we have already stopped listening. It keeps counting down, calm and perfect, while our real plan spreads quietly through the ship. If we want Earth to hear us first, we have to move before the clock reaches zero.

When the countdown reaches its final hours, the ship starts preparing for the official recording. We prepare for ours. Quietly. Carefully. No dramatic speeches, because dramatic speeches echo, and we cannot afford that right now. The microphones are waiting in their case, marked with the same colors as the trails behind our ship. Jiroh rolls in the outfits. Depimi warms up under her voice. Lebang keeps watching the door like he can hear trouble coming before it arrives. Nobody says it, but we all know. If we are doing this, we have to do it now.

The moment the track starts, there is no space left for doubt. The ship becomes our stage, the stars become our backdrop, and every secret hour we spent building this finally turns into sound. Jiroh hits the first line like he has been waiting to cut through the silence. Depimi’s voice lifts the whole thing higher, bright and fearless. Lebang drives the movement forward until the room feels too small to hold us. This is not the clean, empty version they planned. This is louder, stranger, warmer. This is ours, and we give it everything.

For about three seconds after the performance ends, nobody moves. Then the ship notices. Lights snap on across the walls, doors begin locking one by one, and the polite system voice suddenly sounds much less polite. We run. Depimi holds the album tight against her chest while Jiroh keeps checking the route ahead and Lebang forces the slowing doors open before they can seal. Every hallway feels longer than it did yesterday. The songs are recorded, the story is inside, but none of it matters if it stays trapped with us. The shuttle bay is close. Too close to miss.

The shuttle launches before the system can stop it. We stay behind the glass, barely breathing, watching the tiny capsule pull away from our ship and fall toward Earth. Inside it is everything we were told to smooth out, simplify, and hide. Our songs. Our stories. Our proof that first contact does not have to sound like a script. For a moment, it looks impossibly small against the planet below, and we wonder how something so personal can survive such a distance. Then the shuttle keeps going. Somewhere down there, someone is going to find us.

If you are reading this, then it made it. Somewhere on your planet, our album has landed safely, carrying everything we were brave enough to send. The songs, the stories, the pieces of us they tried to make smaller. We do not know who finds it first, or what you will think when you open it. Maybe it will feel strange. Maybe it should. This is our first hello, made by our own hands, sent across the dark because we wanted Earth to know us honestly. We hope you listen. We hope you stay. Thank you for finding JIDLI.

jiroh.text: Hiii, I see you’ve decided to read more into my story. I’m honored, truly. That planet right there, the blue one floating so quietly in all that darkness? That is where I’m from. It might look a little like your Earth from this far away, but trust me, it feels different once you land. The air hums, the nights shine, and even the streets seem to have their own rhythm. People there learn early how to become what others expect. I did too, for a while. Anyway, stay close. I’ll show you the planet first, then me properly.

I grew up in the biggest city on the planet, which sounds impressive until you live inside it. Everything moves fast there: lights, voices, rumors, expectations. Some days it feels like the whole city is leaning over my shoulder, waiting to see what shape I choose next. When it gets too loud, I come up here. Dramatic place to hide, I know. But the rooftop gives me space to breathe and watch the world without entering it. Maybe that sounds lonely. Sometimes it is. But sometimes, from up here, the city almost looks like it could be mine.

I never really felt like myself in my own skin. Weird sentence, I know, but maybe you understand it. I would look in the mirror and feel like something was almost right, just not close enough. So I started changing what I could. First little things: sleeves, collars, cuts only I noticed. Then I learned to sew for real, and fabric stopped being fabric. It became possibility. My clothes became proof that I could choose my own outline. Not what they expected, but what felt honest. When I sit at this machine, I am finally translating me.

I was in bed when the announcement arrived. No warning, just my screen lighting up my face like it knew something I didn’t. The world leaders had found a planet called Earth, and apparently, they had been watching it for a while. Its music, its stages, its idols. The message said every sixteen-year-old had to report to the main temple for selection. Three of us would be chosen to travel there and make first contact. I read it twice, then once more, hoping it would change. My room suddenly felt smaller. The world had found a way inside after all.

My sister messaged me before I had even moved from bed. Just three words: I’m coming over. She knows me too well, annoying talent honestly. The announcement was not a question. Every sixteen-year-old had to report, which meant my suitcase needed answers before I had any. By the time she arrived, I had packed one sock, three shirts, and zero courage. She just sat beside me and folded like the universe could wait. Before closing the suitcase, she slipped in a travel-sized sewing kit. “For emergencies,” she said. I think she knew I was the emergency, maybe already.

And then there I was, standing in front of the temple stairs with my suitcase in my hand and my heart somewhere near my throat. This place was supposed to become my home for the next week. Training, tests, cameras, people watching everything. I still could not believe my life had moved without asking me first. Part of me hoped they would look right through me and choose someone brighter. Another part hated that I wanted to disappear again. So I told myself one thing: survive the week. Just one week. Surely I could do that much, right? Maybe.

That first day felt endless. New faces, new rules, and a trainee hanbok that looked beautiful on everyone except me. It was not ugly. It just felt like wearing someone else’s answer. When the room finally went quiet, I opened the sewing kit my sister packed and sat by the window. I told myself it was only a small adjustment. One careful cut, a few stitches, and the top became cropped, separate from the rest. A two-piece. Mine. The moonlight made it look almost official. For the first time that day, I breathed like I was still in there.

Singing class was one of the first lessons that made me feel exposed. I always liked singing at home, but only when nobody else was there. Not seriously, just making up melodies, changing lyrics, laughing when a note came out weird. In the recording room, the microphone made every breath sound important. My vocal teacher asked for something simple first: no perfect face, no big performance, just sound. I expected to freeze. Instead, my voice came out nervous, then brighter, then almost playful. I was not suddenly a professional, obviously, but singing in front of someone felt possible for me.

Dance class was worse, at least at the start. The room looked older than every fear I brought with me: wooden floors, quiet walls, too much space to hide badly. Our dance instructor played the track and asked us to feel the rhythm before counting it. I moved like I was apologizing for having a body. Then she told me to trust my hips instead of my head. Embarrassing, yes, but she was right. The choreography fit me in the turn, the drop, the sway I usually made smaller. I was sweating, shaking, and finally taking up space on purpose.

Visual class sounded terrifying at first. Apparently, we had to learn how to look natural in front of a camera, which is funny, because nothing feels natural once someone points a lens at you. They gave me a soft white outfit, though, and honestly, thank the stars for that. It felt like something I might have chosen myself. The photographer told me to relax, so I laughed because, sure, easy. But then I actually did. For a few minutes, I forgot to protect every angle of myself. The camera kept clicking, and somehow, I did not want to hide.

As you can see, I had been busy. The final presentation was coming, and everyone had to wear the same trainee hanbok. Cute idea, terrible for me. After everything this week, I could not walk on stage looking like I had simply followed instructions. So I reimagined mine completely. I cut more than I probably should have, separated the pieces, added movement, added shape, added myself. Seeing it hang there felt unreal, like proof I had survived the week in my own language. I was nervous, obviously. But mostly, I could not wait to wear it.

The next morning, I crossed the bridge toward the building where the final evaluation would happen. Water moved quietly underneath me, which felt rude, honestly, because nothing inside me was quiet at all. I wore the outfit I had made with my own hands, and from far away, I probably looked calm. Maybe even confident. Inside? Absolute disaster. My stomach was folding itself into shapes I definitely did not design. Still, I kept walking. The long pieces caught the air behind me, and for one second, they looked braver than I felt. I borrowed that bravery, barely.

Backstage is not really backstage. It is a corner of the courtyard hidden behind black curtains, cables, monitors, and people speaking into headsets. Hands adjust my mic, someone checks my in-ear, someone else says two minutes like that is a normal amount of time to change your life. The final evaluation is being broadcast across the whole planet. Somewhere, my sister might be watching. Somewhere, people who never understood me might be watching too. I keep nodding like the countdown is not getting louder. Beyond the curtain, cameras wait in the light. My fingers touch the mic. Go.

I thought I would feel ready when the music started. Funny, right? The courtyard was quiet in a terrifying way, except for the fire, the cameras, and everyone behind them counting down. Somewhere beyond those lenses, the whole planet could see me. I had written this song from the parts of myself I used to keep private. Every lyric, every step, every breath said: I am here, like this. My fear did not disappear when the track began. It turned into rhythm, into something I could use. I liked how that felt. I hope you like it too.

After the performance, I found a patch of grass and lay down before anyone could ask me how I felt. The moon was above me, almost the same way it looked from my rooftop back home. Funny how far you can travel and still end up staring at the same sky. I kept replaying the week in pieces: the suitcase, the uniform, the first note, the cameras, the song. I think I did well. I think I changed. But now the waiting felt heavier than the performance. What if they chose me? What if part of me wanted that?

Oh my stars. They called my name. For a second, I did not move, because I was sure I had heard wrong. Then everyone around me started reacting, and my body finally caught up with the truth. I had won. I was chosen. The relief hit so hard I laughed before I could stop myself. All that panic, all that trying, all the moments I thought I should disappear, and somehow I had made it through. I looked up at the temple bell above me and thought of my sister. I hope you’re watching, I wanted to tell her. I did it.

After they called my name, I returned to my room and found the package waiting on my bed. Silver wrapping, careful string, a tiny note from Mom, Dad, and Sis. Inside was a personal 3D printer, compact enough to come with me, strong enough to make more than tiny souvenirs. I laughed, then cried, then laughed again because of course they would choose the most practical way to say I love you. Maybe they did not understand every part of me, but they understood this: I needed to keep making. Earth could feel less far with that beside me.

The helmet made everything sound distant: the engines, the countdown, the voice over comms asking if we were ready. Ready still felt like a dramatic word, but I nodded anyway. Through the glass, the stars looked closer than they ever had from my rooftop. I thought about every version of me I was leaving behind: the quiet one, the hidden one, the one who stitched himself back together. They were coming with me, just softer now. Thank you for staying with my story. Now listen to our music and get to know Depimi and Lebang too. We are only beginning together.

depimi.text: Hi, I’m Depimi. You found my story too? That makes me a little nervous, but in a good way, I think. That planet floating there is where I’m from. From this far away, it probably looks calm, almost like it is sleeping. My home is somewhere in the green parts, between mountains, soft clouds, and villages that wake up with the light. Life there moves slowly. People grow flowers, share meals, remember birthdays, and somehow notice every new haircut before sunset. I liked that softness. Maybe too much. It made staying exactly where I was feel very easy.

This is my sisters and me, sitting on the old stone steps near our little house in the forest. The villagers used to say we looked like triplets, even though we were not. I think they meant it as a compliment. Three pretty sisters, always together, always easy to recognize. I never knew what to do with that kind of attention. Beauty felt like something people talked about around me, not something that helped me decide who to become. My sisters had plans. I mostly had quiet. I liked being beside them, matching their pace. Standing out sounded exhausting.

When I wanted to disappear without worrying anyone, I brought my paints outside. There was a quiet place near the rocks where the forest covered most of the sky, and nobody looked for me unless dinner was ready. I could sit there for hours, mixing colors until even my thoughts softened. A loud life never sounded beautiful to me. Still, sometimes, while painting alone, I wondered what it would feel like to have friends who pulled me somewhere unexpected. Not away from myself. Just gently past the edge of my little world, where something new might finally find me.

I was walking through the forest when the announcement cut through the trees. At first, I thought it was sunlight, strange and sharp between the leaves. Then the message opened in the air, too bright for such a quiet place. The world leaders had found a planet called Earth, and they had been watching it for a while. Its music, its stages, its idols. Every sixteen-year-old had to report to the main temple for selection. Three of us would travel there and make first contact. My hands went cold. My little world had been opened without knocking, all at once.

My sisters found me before I could pretend I was fine. Of course they did. I had been sitting there for so long the lanterns had turned warm around us, but my thoughts still felt cold. I did not want a brave speech. I wanted the house, the forest, our usual evening sounds. I wanted everything to stay where I knew how to love it. My sisters understood that, I think. The sketchbook in my hands felt like a small door. Empty, quiet, patient. I was not ready to leave. But maybe I could start by drawing what waited outside.

So there I stood, in front of the place that was supposed to be my home for the next week. Every bone in my body wanted to turn around, walk back down the path, and pretend the announcement had never found me. Maybe I should have. Maybe I could have hidden so well nobody would think to look. But deep down, I knew that was not really an option. The world had already changed shape around me. I could either freeze at the edge of it, or step inside before someone had to push me. I hated that I stepped.

That night, I found a tree behind the temple and sat under it with my sketchbook. I was supposed to sleep, but my mind kept replaying the day in sharp little pieces: names, rooms, instructions, people moving too quickly. Drawing was the only way I knew how to make things slow down. Line by line, the temple stopped feeling like something huge above me and became shapes I could hold on paper. The moon helped. So did the quiet. I was still scared of tomorrow’s first real lesson, but at least my hands remembered how to be calm.

Dance class was the first lesson that made me realize talent has different languages. The instructor played a fast pop track, all sharp counts and sudden hits, and everyone around me seemed to understand it instantly. My body did not. It kept reaching for the movements I knew from home: slow turns, soft wrists, steps that followed wind instead of beats. I tried to force myself into the choreography, but the harder I pushed, the stranger I felt. It was frustrating, almost embarrassing. I had rhythm, just not this kind. My body was speaking softly in a room that wanted impact.

Visual class should have been easy for me, at least that is what everyone seemed to think. Sit there, smile softly, look pretty, let the camera find your best angle. I knew how to be looked at when my sisters were beside me. Alone, it felt different. The photographer kept saying perfect, beautiful, again, and I kept doing what she asked. The strange part was that it worked. The pictures looked gentle, bright, almost effortless. Everyone seemed pleased. I smiled back because that was easier than explaining how lonely it felt to be admired without anyone holding my hand.

Singing class surprised me. I thought it would feel like another place where I had to become louder, brighter, bigger than myself. Instead, the room became quiet when I started. My voice came out high and thin at first, almost afraid of its own shape. Then the teacher asked me not to push it, just let it float. So I did. The note lifted softly, clear enough to make everyone listen without forcing them to. For the first time that week, I did not feel like my softness was something to overcome. It sounded like the best part of me.

That night, I opened my sketchbook and drew my sisters from memory. I thought it would make me miss them less, which was a very silly thing to believe. Their faces appeared slowly, line by line, and the room felt too quiet without their voices filling it. But this time, missing them did not make me want to run home. It made me want to return with something beautiful to show them. Something brave. I traced their faces one last time and promised, quietly, that I would try to become someone they could be proud of someday, somehow.

That new confidence did not last as long as I hoped. The next morning, I got ready for the final presentation, and every brave thought from the night before suddenly sounded far away. The walk there was peaceful, which almost made it worse. Water under the bridge, trees moving softly, everything calm except me. I kept thinking: What if I forget the steps? What if my voice disappears? What if everyone can tell I do not belong here? I tried to breathe slowly, tried to look composed, but inside I was panicking in every direction. Still, I crossed.

Before the performance, someone fixed my makeup while I stared at myself in the mirror. It felt strange seeing only my own face there, not part of a pair, not blended into anyone else’s shadow, just me. I looked softer than I felt. Prettier than I felt. Braver than I felt, maybe. The room moved around me in little flashes: lights, brushes, voices, countdowns. I wanted to shrink into something smaller, but the mirror would not let me. So I stayed there, looking back. For the first time, I tried to believe that one face could be enough.

When the music started, I expected my fear to take over. Instead, something quiet inside me opened. I moved carefully at first, then sharper, letting the soft turns from home meet the pop rhythm I had fought so hard to understand. My voice lifted clear above the firelight, and the cameras came closer like they wanted to know my secret. I could feel everyone watching through the lenses, but this time it did not swallow me. I held their attention gently, almost without trying. Maybe that was my power. Not demanding the spotlight, but making it stay with me.

After the performance, I found the water before anyone could find me. My reflection kept moving, softer and brighter than I expected, and I could not stop looking at her. A week ago, I would have sworn that girl did not exist. The one who sang clearly. The one who held the cameras. The one who moved through fear and somehow made it beautiful. I was still nervous about the results, of course, but underneath that, something warm was blooming. I had done well. I knew it. For the first time, being proud of myself did not feel impossible.

When they called my name, I laughed before I even understood why. It came out bright and messy, completely unlike the quiet reaction I imagined for myself. I had won. I had actually won. For a moment, I could not think about Earth, or the mission, or what came next. I just stood there under the temple bell, feeling proud in a way that reached every part of me. The girl who wanted to stay small had crossed the bridge, faced the cameras, and found her voice. I was still soft. I was just stronger than I knew.

After everything, I went back to my room and found a package waiting outside the door. The note said it was from Mom and my sisters, and somehow that was enough to make the whole hallway feel like home. Inside was a drawing tablet, smooth and new, with more empty space than any sketchbook could ever hold. I laughed because of course they knew. Paper runs out. Paint dries. Pencils break. But they wanted me to keep making, wherever I went next. I held it close for a moment. Not because it was expensive. Because it meant they understood.

Inside the spaceship, everything was too clean, too quiet, too far from the forest. I kept my new tablet close, like holding it could make the distance smaller. The comms asked if we were ready, and I almost smiled. I was not sure I would ever feel ready in the way brave people do. But I had learned something this week: I could be scared and still move. I could be soft and still leave a mark. Thank you for walking through my story with me. Listen to our music, and get to know Jiroh and Lebang too.

lebang.text: Hey, I’m Lebang. That blue planet over there? That’s home. Not one exact place, though. I grew up on the water, close to the shorelines where the land turns green and the ocean decides most of your plans. Some mornings were bright enough to make the whole sea look alive. Other days, the waves got loud and reminded everyone who was really in charge. I liked that. The water never pretended to be still just to make people comfortable. Maybe that is why I never learned how to stay in one place for too long either.

My brother raised me more than he should have had to. Our parents were lost at sea when I was young, and after that, he became the closest thing I had to a map. He taught me how to tie knots, read clouds, fix broken things, and pray even when the water stayed quiet. I think I learned strength from watching him pretend he was never tired. Maybe that is why I wanted to become someone he could be proud of. Someone useful. Someone brave. But sometimes being strong felt less like courage and more like never asking to rest.

I was never good at sitting with heavy thoughts. If something hurt, I moved. Surfing helped with that. Out on the water, there was no space for pretending, no room for slow sadness. You either listened to the wave or got swallowed by it. Simple. Honest. A little rude sometimes. I liked anything that made my heart wake up: swimming, climbing, racing storms, chasing the next impossible thing before fear caught up. Maybe that sounds reckless. I called it practice. Life was too fragile to watch from the shore. I wanted to meet it already moving.

I was under the palms when the announcement appeared above the trees. For a second, I thought it was lightning without thunder. Then the message sharpened. The world leaders had found a planet called Earth, and they had been watching it for a while. Its music, its stages, its idols. Every sixteen-year-old had to report to the main temple for selection. Three of us would travel there and make first contact. I had always wanted the world to get bigger. I just never imagined it would happen like an order. Or that leaving meant leaving my brother alone.

My brother found me before I could fully disappear into my own head. I was excited, honestly. A real mission. Another planet. A chance to see something bigger than every shoreline I had ever chased. But every time that excitement rose, guilt pulled it back down. Leaving felt different when the person you were leaving was the only family you had. We sat near the fire for a while, quiet in the way only brothers can be. Then he handed me brand new dancing shoes. “Take them,” he said. “If you go, go with both feet.” I laughed, but it stayed with me.

So there I stood, in front of the place that was supposed to be my home for the next week. The temple looked unreal up close, like something built for people who already knew their purpose. I had been excited on the way there, honestly. New place, new faces, a challenge big enough to make my pulse wake up. But standing at the bottom of those stairs, the excitement got heavier. This was not just an adventure anymore. It was a test. I rolled my shoulders back, grabbed my suitcase tighter, and told myself to enter like I meant it.

After the first day, my head felt full of other people’s voices. Instructions, names, footsteps, doors sliding open and shut. Too many new things at once. So when I found the natural pool behind the temple, I got in before I could talk myself out of it. It was not the ocean. It did not pull or roar or challenge me. But the water still understood me better than the floor did. I floated there until the day stopped clinging to me, one ripple at a time.

Visual class humbled me fast. I thought standing in front of a camera would be simple: sit still, look decent, do not blink weirdly. Apparently, that is not a strategy. The photographer kept asking me to relax, which made every muscle in my body immediately do the opposite. I could feel my shoulders locking, my face getting serious, my hands forgetting where hands normally go. The pictures were not terrible, I guess. I looked fine. Maybe even good from far away. But they did not feel like me. They felt like proof that confidence can disappear on command.

Singing class surprised me in a quieter way. I did not walk in expecting much, just hoping my voice would not betray me the second the room went silent. The vocal teacher asked for a few simple lines first, nothing dramatic, just enough to hear where I naturally sat. My voice came out low, steady, warmer than I thought it would. Not perfect, not polished, but real. The kind of sound I could lean on. I remember opening my eyes after the last note and thinking, okay. Maybe there was more in me than motion and muscle.

Dance class felt like someone had finally switched the language back to one I could speak. The instructor showed the combination once, and my body caught it before my brain finished taking notes. Hit, turn, drop, hold. It made sense. The shoes my brother gave me gripped the floor like they had been waiting for this room too. I did not have to fake confidence here. I just moved, and the movement answered back. By the end, I was breathing hard, smiling harder, and trying not to look too proud of myself. But I was. More than a little.

The night before the final presentation, I made a paper boat and set it on the temple pond. It was a little childish, maybe, but it helped. Watching it drift between the lantern reflections made everything feel less far away: my brother, our boat, the water I knew by heart. I thought about the week, the classes, the shoes, the way my body had started answering before my doubts could. Tomorrow still mattered. A lot. But for once, I did not feel like I had to force courage into myself. I already had enough to begin with.

The next morning, the walk to the final presentation was almost too peaceful for me. The bridge curved over quiet water, the garden held its breath, and the whole place seemed determined to move slowly. That was the problem. Slow gave my thoughts too much space. I was used to burning through nerves with movement, turning pressure into something my body could understand before it became doubt. Here, I had to carry it calmly, step after step, like a flame I was not allowed to drop. By the time I reached the other side, I was ready to release it..

Backstage, I tied my shoes tighter than necessary. The knot was already fine, but my hands needed a job, and the countdown kept getting closer. Around me, cables dragged across the floor, people checked headsets, and someone rolled a case past like this was all normal. I thought about my brother laughing at me for looking so serious. That helped. A little. The final evaluation was being broadcast across the planet, but I tried not to imagine every screen. I pressed my foot against the floor, felt the grip, and told myself one thing: move like you mean it now.

When the music hit, the pressure finally had somewhere to go. My voice came out low and steady, stronger than it had in practice, and my body followed before I had time to think. Every step felt grounded. Every hit landed clean. I was not trying to look dangerous or become some version of a pop star that did not fit me. I was just letting the fire move through me instead of holding it back. The cameras stayed close, the flames rose around the courtyard, and for once, I did not chase the moment. I stood inside it.

After the performance, I found the greenhouse and sat where the air felt warmer. I knew I had done well. That was not the problem, which somehow made everything more complicated. My body was still full of leftover energy, but my thoughts had gone quiet enough to hear the question underneath it all. What if they chose me? What if I had earned the one thing that would take me farther from my brother than any tide ever could? I looked at the plants around me, steady and rooted, and wondered if wanting something could still hurt this much.

When they called my name, I froze for half a second. Then it hit me all at once. I had won. I had actually won. The laugh came out before I could stop it, wide and stupid and probably not very graceful, but I did not care. I had given everything, and somehow it had been enough. The temple bell hung above me, quiet and huge, like it had been waiting for this exact moment. I thought of my brother first. I hoped he was watching. I hoped he was smiling. Then I let myself be proud.

When I got back, the gift was waiting by the shoe cubbies, right where I would not miss it. From big bro, the note said, like he was trying to sound casual and failing. Inside was a small speaker, sturdy enough to survive travel and loud enough to annoy a whole spaceship if I wanted. Under it, he had written: for practice, since you clearly still need it. I laughed so hard the room stopped feeling empty. Of course he would turn goodbye into a joke. But I knew what he meant. Keep singing. Keep dancing. Keep going.

Inside the spaceship, the helmet made my own breathing sound closer than the engines. I thought leaving would feel clean somehow, like crossing a line and becoming someone new on the other side. It did not. Part of me was still back there, with the water, my brother, and the life that made me. I was sadder than I expected. But under that sadness, something bright kept moving. Earth was ahead of us, full of places I had never seen and stories I had not earned yet. Thank you for coming this far. Get to know Jiroh and Depimi too.
